topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into my otherwise mundane Tuesday. *For Sale: Charming Bungalow with Mature Trees.* My jaw tightened as I stared at the listing photo—my childhood home, the place where scraped knees healed and secrets were whispered under the eaves. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my chest. I clicked on the details, the familiar layout of the rooms mocking me. Why now? Who would want this place? I scrolled through the price, a number that felt impossibly high, a blatant disrespect to the history etched into every peeling paint chip. I slammed the phone face down on the kitchen counter, the sound sharp and brittle. I needed a distraction, anything to push this unwelcome reality away.

[story 2]

I was making coffee, the ritual a necessary anchor in the swirling chaos of my morning, when the email landed. It was from a real estate aggregator, and the subject line read, “Your Old Address, Re-listed.” My hand, mid-pour, froze. The steam from the mug seemed to hang in the air, thick and suffocating. I set the ceramic down with a deliberate, almost violent clink. A tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, squeezing the air from my lungs. I leaned against the counter, my gaze fixed on the screen, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the granite. This was absurd. It was a cruel joke played by some unseen cosmic entity. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small kitchen, the floorboards protesting under my hurried steps.

[story 3]

It was late, the kind of late where the silence of the apartment amplifies every internal dissonance. I was scrolling aimlessly through social media when the listing caught my eye—the address. A sudden, sharp jolt ran down my spine, followed by a deep, unsettling weariness settling over my shoulders. I reread the description three times, my eyes scanning the familiar details with a detached, almost hostile scrutiny. A low growl escaped my throat, a sound I immediately tried to swallow back down. I walked over to the window, pulling the heavy curtain aside just enough to look out at the anonymous street, feeling a profound sense of being invaded, as if a memory had been forcibly unearthed and put on display for public consumption.
